catalyst.llm generation: mesostructured CeO 2 graphite carbon nitride (m-CeO 2 g-C 3 N 4 )
catalyst.source: Herein, mesostructured CeO 2 graphite carbon nitride (m-CeO 2 g-C 3 N 4 ) was designed and synthesized through a hard-template route.
catalyst.context: CO 2 is highly stable and therefore extremely difficult to be reduced at room temperature.Herein, mesostructured CeO 2 graphite carbon nitride (m-CeO 2 g-C 3 N 4 ) was designed and synthesized through a hard-template route.The heterogeneous nanocomposites showed greatly enhanced response to solar light, promoted charge carrier separation and transfer efficiency.Consequently, their CO 2 photoreduction performance has been remarkably enhanced.Maximum CO and CH 4 yields of 0.590 and 0.694 mol, respectively, have been obtained from the CO 2 reduction after one hour irradiation at room temperature on 50 mg nanocomposite photocatalyst.Different from common g-C 3 N 4 -based composites in which the additional component only plays a role of electron sink in electron-hole separation, a synergetic effect of mutual activations between the two components is proposed, which is featured with g-C 3 N 4 activation due to significantly promoted separation of photo-generated carriers under Xenon lamp irradiation, and CeO 2 activation via the reduction of Ce 4 to Ce 3 by the trapped electrons.

catalyst.source: The as-synthesized nanocomposites are denoted as x m-CeO 2 g-C 3 N 4 .
catalyst.context: All the chemicals are analytical grade and used as received without further purification.The nanocomposites m-CeO 2 g-C 3 N 4 were prepared as follows: 20.0 g urea and certain amount (x wt) of Ce(NO 3 ) 3 6H 2 O were dissolved in 45.0 g water dispersion with 40 12-nm SiO 2 particles (Ludox HS-40, Aldrich), which were used as hard template.The mixture was kept at 85 1C with magnetic stirring to evaporate water and then put in an oven at 80 1C overnight.The resultant white powder was calcined at 550 1C in an alumina crucible with a cover for 2 h in static air with a ramp rate of 4 1Cmin.The yellow product was finally treated with 4 M ammonium bifluoride (NH 4 HF 2 ) for 48 h to remove the silica template.The powder was then centrifuged and washed with ethanol and distilled water for several times and lyophilized overnight.The as-synthesized nanocomposites are denoted as x m-CeO 2 g-C 3 N 4 .The mesoporous g-C 3 N 4 (m-g-C 3 N 4 ) was prepared by the same method without adding Ce(NO 3 ) 3 6H 2 O.The mesoporous CeO 2 (m-CeO 2 ) can be obtained by removing g-C 3 N 4 from the m-CeO 2 g-C 3 N 4 nanocomposite under 550 1C for 3 h, as demonstrated by XPS analysis below. For photocatalyst characterization, X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD) were recorded by a Rigaku DMax 2200PC X-ray diffractometer with a scanning rate of 4 1min.The X-ray tube using Cu K radiation was operated at 40 mA and 40 kV.The measurement were made at room temperature in the 2 range of 10-801.Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area of the samples was characterized by sorption using Micromeritics Tristar 2000 instrument at 77 K. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns, and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of the catalyst samples were obtained by JEM-2100F field emission transmission electron microscope (FE-TEM) operated at 200 kV and equipped with an energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) instrument.Field emission scanning electron micrograph (FE-SEM) was acquired on a Hitachi S-4800.Ultraviolet visible (UV-vis) diffuse reflection spectra were recorded in the range of 200-800 nm on a UV-3101 PC Shimadzu spectroscope using BaSO 4 as a reflectance standard material.A Hitachi F-4500 was used to analyze the catalysts' photoluminescence (PL) characteristics with an excitation wavelength of 325 nm at room temperature.X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out on a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250 spectrometer with an Al K radiation line source and multichannel detector.All the binding energy values for the high-resolution spectra were calibrated with the signal of contaminated carbon C 1 s at 285 eV. Electrochemical measurements were carried out on a CHI 660A electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument, Inc.) with a standard three-electrode cell.Fluorine-doped Tin Oxide (FTO) deposited with photocatalysts served as a working electrode, while the counter and the reference electrodes were a platinum plate and AgAgCl respectively, with 0.2 M Na 2 SO 4 as electrolyte solution.The working electrodes were prepared by electrophoretic deposition in an acetone solution (25 mL) containing the sample powder (12 mg) and iodine (3 mg).Two parallel FTO glasses were kept in the above solution for 5 min with a 10 V bias under potentiostat control.All the samples are deposited as a thin film onto Fluorine-doped Tin Oxide (FTO), and the coated area was fixed at 1 cm 2 .After being calcined for 2 h in an oven at 150 1C, a homogeneous film can be obtained.50 mL of 0.2 M Na 2 SO 4 was used as electrolyte solution, which represented the best compromise between activity and stability of the nanocomposites.The transient photocurrent densities of the as-prepared electrodes were tested at 0.3 V versus AgAgCl under irradiation.The bias voltage for obtaining Nyquist plots of these electrodes was set at A 0.4 V versus AgAgCl in dark. CO 2 photocatalytic reduction was typically conducted in a gas-closed circulation system by using a reactor (volume: 500 mL) purchased from PerfectLight Company (China, Beijing).The reactor is made of borosilicate glass, which was purchased from Perfect Light Company (China, Beijing), with an optical quartz window at the top.To remove possible trace organic contaminants, all the samples were treated at 200 1C for 3 h in air.In a typical process, 50 mg photocatalyst was uniformly dispersed on a flat glass plate with the area fixed at 1.5 cm A 1.5 cm.In order to ensure that air was eliminated, high purity CO 2 (99.99) gas, monitoreded by a mass flow controller, was led to pass through a water bubbler to generate a mixture of CO 2 and water vapor.A 300 W of Xenon-arc lamp (Aulight CEL-HX, Beijing) was used as the light source.The temperature of the reactor was controlled at 25 1C by cooling water circulation.During the irradiation, 1 mL gas was sampled from the glass chamber at a given time intervals (1 h) for detecting CH 4 andor other carbon-based gases.CO can also be detected by installing a methanizer, using N 2 as carrier gas.The sample was analyzed by a gas chromatograph (GC-2060, Fuli Corp., China) with a flame ionization detector (FID) equipped with a 5A molecular column and a TDX-01 packed column.During the stability test, the photocatalyst was collected after each run and then reused for photoreduction of CO 2 .The catalyst was heattreated at 150 1C for two hours after the second and third runs before its reusing.

catalyst.source: This work presents a promising m-CeO 2 g-C 3 N 4 photocatalyst for CO 2 photoconversion without addition of any noble-metal co-catalyst.
catalyst.context: In conclusion, we have demonstrated a novel kind of mesostructured CeO 2 g-C 3 N 4 nanocomposites by a simple hard template method, which exhibit significantly enhanced Fig. 1 SEM images of (a) m-g-C 3 N 4 , (b) 3 m-CeO 2 g-C 3 N 4 nanocomposite; TEM images of (c) m-g-C 3 N 4 , (d) m-CeO 2 , (e) 3 m-CeO 2 g-C 3 N 4 nanocomposite with inserted SAED pattern (inset of e); (f) HR-TEM image of 3 m-CeO 2 g-C 3 N 4 sample. Fig. 2 (a) XRD patterns of m-CeO 2 , m-g-C 3 N 4 , and m-CeO 2 g-C 3 N 4 samples; (b) N 2 adsorption-desorption isotherms; (c) corresponding pore-size distributions calculated from the desorption branch of the as-prepared samples. Fig. 3 (a) UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS); (b) the magnified DRS image of the selected frame from (a); (c) the plots of (ahv) 12 versus energy (hv) for the band gap energies of the samples; (d) photoluminescence spectra of the as-prepared samples excited at 325 nm. Fig. 4 (a) Transient photocurrent density of m-CeO 2 , m-g-C 3 N 4 , and 3 m-CeO 2 g-C 3 N 4 electrodes at 0.3 V versus AgAgCl; (b) electrochemical impedance spectra of the as-prepared samples at A 0.4 V versus AgAgCl. Fig. 5 XPS profiles of (a) survey, (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, and (d) Ce 3d of the samples m-CeO 2 , m-g-C 3 N 4 and 3 m-CeO 2 g-C 3 N 4 . Fig. 6 Time-dependent CO (a), CH 4 (b) amounts generated on m-CeO 2 , m-g-C 3 N 4 , and m-CeO 2 g-C 3 N 4 nanocomposites.Stability studies of CO (c) and CH 4 (d) evolutions over 3 m-CeO 2 g-C 3 N 4 .Condition: 50 mg catalyst, reaction temperature 25 1C, ambient pressure, 300 W Xe-lamp. light response, highly efficient separation and transfer of photogenerated charge carriers across the interface between mesoporous CeO 2 and g-C 3 N 4 , and consequently, the greatly enhanced photocatalytic performance in the photoreduction of CO 2 by gaseous water under full spectrum light irradiation.A synergetic catalytic effect of mutual activations between m-CeO 2 and g-C 3 N 4 is proposed: The migration of photo-generated electrons from g-C 3 N 4 to m-CeO 2 and their trapping by Ce 4 leads to the recombination prevention of photogenerated electrons and holes in g-C 3 N 4 (g-C 3 N 4 activation) and the generation of Ce 3 from Ce 4 reduction by the trapped electrons in m-CeO 2 (CeO 2 activation), both of which benefits the activity enhancements of CO 2 reduction.As a result, as high as 0.590 mol CO and 0.694 mol CH 4 can be obtained by CO 2 reduction on 50 mg photocatalyst in the first hour.This work presents a promising m-CeO 2 g-C 3 N 4 photocatalyst for CO 2 photoconversion without addition of any noble-metal co-catalyst.Mengli Li received her M.S. degree from Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2010.Currently she is a Ph.D. candidate under the supervision of Professor Jianlin Shi at Shanghai Institute of Ceramics, Chinese Academy of Sciences.Her current main research focuses on the design and synthesis of graphitic carbon nitride based nanocomposites used in environmental protection and energy conversion.Lingxia Zhang received her Ph.D. degree from Shanghai Institute of Ceramics, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2003.She has been working at the institute after graduation.Currently her research mainly includes mesoporous and low-dimensional materials applied in artificial photosynthesis and environmental purification. Physicochemical properties and photocatalytic activities of the photocatalyst for CO 2 reduction under simulated solar light. a BET surface area.bBJHaverageporesize.cTheamount of CO evolution after 1 h irradiation.dTheamount of CH 4 evolution after 1 h irradiation. This work was financially supported by the National Key Basic Research Program of China (2013CB933200), National 863 plans projects (2012AA062703), National Natural Science Foundation of China (21177137), Youth Innovation Promotion Association CAS (2012200) and Shanghai Technical Platform for Testing and Characterization on Inorganic Materials (14DZ2292900). Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at http:dx.doi.org10.1016j.nanoen.2015.11.010.

lamp.source: A 300 W of Xenon-arc lamp (Aulight CEL-HX, Beijing) was used as the light source.
lamp.context: All the chemicals are analytical grade and used as received without further purification.The nanocomposites m-CeO 2 g-C 3 N 4 were prepared as follows: 20.0 g urea and certain amount (x wt) of Ce(NO 3 ) 3 6H 2 O were dissolved in 45.0 g water dispersion with 40 12-nm SiO 2 particles (Ludox HS-40, Aldrich), which were used as hard template.The mixture was kept at 85 1C with magnetic stirring to evaporate water and then put in an oven at 80 1C overnight.The resultant white powder was calcined at 550 1C in an alumina crucible with a cover for 2 h in static air with a ramp rate of 4 1Cmin.The yellow product was finally treated with 4 M ammonium bifluoride (NH 4 HF 2 ) for 48 h to remove the silica template.The powder was then centrifuged and washed with ethanol and distilled water for several times and lyophilized overnight.The as-synthesized nanocomposites are denoted as x m-CeO 2 g-C 3 N 4 .The mesoporous g-C 3 N 4 (m-g-C 3 N 4 ) was prepared by the same method without adding Ce(NO 3 ) 3 6H 2 O.The mesoporous CeO 2 (m-CeO 2 ) can be obtained by removing g-C 3 N 4 from the m-CeO 2 g-C 3 N 4 nanocomposite under 550 1C for 3 h, as demonstrated by XPS analysis below. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns, and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of the catalyst samples were obtained by JEM-2100F field emission transmission electron microscope (FE-TEM) operated at 200 kV and equipped with an energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) instrument.Field emission scanning electron micrograph (FE-SEM) was acquired on a Hitachi S-4800.Ultraviolet visible (UV-vis) diffuse reflection spectra were recorded in the range of 200-800 nm on a UV-3101 PC Shimadzu spectroscope using BaSO 4 as a reflectance standard material.A Hitachi F-4500 was used to analyze the catalysts' photoluminescence (PL) characteristics with an excitation wavelength of 325 nm at room temperature.X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out on a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250 spectrometer with an Al K radiation line source and multichannel detector.All the binding energy values for the high-resolution spectra were calibrated with the signal of contaminated carbon C 1 s at 285 eV. Electrochemical measurements were carried out on a CHI 660A electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument, Inc.) with a standard three-electrode cell.Fluorine-doped Tin Oxide (FTO) deposited with photocatalysts served as a working electrode, while the counter and the reference electrodes were a platinum plate and AgAgCl respectively, with 0.2 M Na 2 SO 4 as electrolyte solution.The working electrodes were prepared by electrophoretic deposition in an acetone solution (25 mL) containing the sample powder (12 mg) and iodine (3 mg).Two parallel FTO glasses were kept in the above solution for 5 min with a 10 V bias under potentiostat control.All the samples are deposited as a thin film onto Fluorine-doped Tin Oxide (FTO), and the coated area was fixed at 1 cm 2 .After being calcined for 2 h in an oven at 150 1C, a homogeneous film can be obtained.50 mL of 0.2 M Na 2 SO 4 was used as electrolyte solution, which represented the best compromise between activity and stability of the nanocomposites.The transient photocurrent densities of the as-prepared electrodes were tested at 0.3 V versus AgAgCl under irradiation.The bias voltage for obtaining Nyquist plots of these electrodes was set at A 0.4 V versus AgAgCl in dark. CO 2 photocatalytic reduction was typically conducted in a gas-closed circulation system by using a reactor (volume: 500 mL) purchased from PerfectLight Company (China, Beijing).The reactor is made of borosilicate glass, which was purchased from Perfect Light Company (China, Beijing), with an optical quartz window at the top.To remove possible trace organic contaminants, all the samples were treated at 200 1C for 3 h in air.In a typical process, 50 mg photocatalyst was uniformly dispersed on a flat glass plate with the area fixed at 1.5 cm A 1.5 cm.In order to ensure that air was eliminated, high purity CO 2 (99.99) gas, monitoreded by a mass flow controller, was led to pass through a water bubbler to generate a mixture of CO 2 and water vapor.A 300 W of Xenon-arc lamp (Aulight CEL-HX, Beijing) was used as the light source.The temperature of the reactor was controlled at 25 1C by cooling water circulation.During the irradiation, 1 mL gas was sampled from the glass chamber at a given time intervals (1 h) for detecting CH 4 andor other carbon-based gases.CO can also be detected by installing a methanizer, using N 2 as carrier gas.The sample was analyzed by a gas chromatograph (GC-2060, Fuli Corp., China) with a flame ionization detector (FID) equipped with a 5A molecular column and a TDX-01 packed column.During the stability test, the photocatalyst was collected after each run and then reused for photoreduction of CO 2 .The catalyst was heattreated at 150 1C for two hours after the second and third runs before its reusing.

lamp.source: Condition: 50 mg catalyst, reaction temperature 25 1C, ambient pressure, 300 W Xe-lamp.

For photocatalyst characterization, X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD) were recorded by a Rigaku DMax 2200PC X-ray diffractometer with a scanning rate of 4 1min.The X-ray tube using Cu K radiation was operated at 40 mA and 40 kV.The measurement were made at room temperature in the 2 range of 10-801.Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area of the samples was characterized by sorption using Micromeritics Tristar 2000 instrument at 77 K. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns, and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of the catalyst samples were obtained by JEM-2100F field emission transmission electron microscope (FE-TEM) operated at 200 kV and equipped with an energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) instrument.Field emission scanning electron micrograph (FE-SEM) was acquired on a Hitachi S-4800.Ultraviolet visible (UV-vis) diffuse reflection spectra were recorded in the range of 200-800 nm on a UV-3101 PC Shimadzu spectroscope using BaSO 4 as a reflectance standard material.A Hitachi F-4500 was used to analyze the catalysts' photoluminescence (PL) characteristics with an excitation wavelength of 325 nm at room temperature.X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out on a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250 spectrometer with an Al K radiation line source and multichannel detector.All the binding energy values for the high-resolution spectra were calibrated with the signal of contaminated carbon C 1 s at 285 eV. Electrochemical measurements were carried out on a CHI 660A electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument, Inc.) with a standard three-electrode cell.Fluorine-doped Tin Oxide (FTO) deposited with photocatalysts served as a working electrode, while the counter and the reference electrodes were a platinum plate and AgAgCl respectively, with 0.2 M Na 2 SO 4 as electrolyte solution.The working electrodes were prepared by electrophoretic deposition in an acetone solution (25 mL) containing the sample powder (12 mg) and iodine (3 mg).Two parallel FTO glasses were kept in the above solution for 5 min with a 10 V bias under potentiostat control.All the samples are deposited as a thin film onto Fluorine-doped Tin Oxide (FTO), and the coated area was fixed at 1 cm 2 .After being calcined for 2 h in an oven at 150 1C, a homogeneous film can be obtained.50 mL of 0.2 M Na 2 SO 4 was used as electrolyte solution, which represented the best compromise between activity and stability of the nanocomposites.The transient photocurrent densities of the as-prepared electrodes were tested at 0.3 V versus AgAgCl under irradiation.The bias voltage for obtaining Nyquist plots of these electrodes was set at A 0.4 V versus AgAgCl in dark. CO 2 photocatalytic reduction was typically conducted in a gas-closed circulation system by using a reactor (volume: 500 mL) purchased from PerfectLight Company (China, Beijing).The reactor is made of borosilicate glass, which was purchased from Perfect Light Company (China, Beijing), with an optical quartz window at the top.To remove possible trace organic contaminants, all the samples were treated at 200 1C for 3 h in air.In a typical process, 50 mg photocatalyst was uniformly dispersed on a flat glass plate with the area fixed at 1.5 cm A 1.5 cm.In order to ensure that air was eliminated, high purity CO 2 (99.99) gas, monitoreded by a mass flow controller, was led to pass through a water bubbler to generate a mixture of CO 2 and water vapor.A 300 W of Xenon-arc lamp (Aulight CEL-HX, Beijing) was used as the light source.The temperature of the reactor was controlled at 25 1C by cooling water circulation.During the irradiation, 1 mL gas was sampled from the glass chamber at a given time intervals (1 h) for detecting CH 4 andor other carbon-based gases.CO can also be detected by installing a methanizer, using N 2 as carrier gas.The sample was analyzed by a gas chromatograph (GC-2060, Fuli Corp., China) with a flame ionization detector (FID) equipped with a 5A molecular column and a TDX-01 packed column.During the stability test, the photocatalyst was collected after each run and then reused for photoreduction of CO 2 .The catalyst was heattreated at 150 1C for two hours after the second and third runs before its reusing.

reaction medium.source: light response, highly efficient separation and transfer of photogenerated charge carriers across the interface between mesoporous CeO 2 and g-C 3 N 4 , and consequently, the greatly enhanced photocatalytic performance in the photoreduction of CO 2 by gaseous water under full spectrum light irradiation.
reaction medium.context: In conclusion, we have demonstrated a novel kind of mesostructured CeO 2 g-C 3 N 4 nanocomposites by a simple hard template method, which exhibit significantly enhanced Fig. 1 SEM images of (a) m-g-C 3 N 4 , (b) 3 m-CeO 2 g-C 3 N 4 nanocomposite; TEM images of (c) m-g-C 3 N 4 , (d) m-CeO 2 , (e) 3 m-CeO 2 g-C 3 N 4 nanocomposite with inserted SAED pattern (inset of e); (f) HR-TEM image of 3 m-CeO 2 g-C 3 N 4 sample. Fig. 2 (a) XRD patterns of m-CeO 2 , m-g-C 3 N 4 , and m-CeO 2 g-C 3 N 4 samples; (b) N 2 adsorption-desorption isotherms; (c) corresponding pore-size distributions calculated from the desorption branch of the as-prepared samples. Fig. 3 (a) UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS); (b) the magnified DRS image of the selected frame from (a); (c) the plots of (ahv) 12 versus energy (hv) for the band gap energies of the samples; (d) photoluminescence spectra of the as-prepared samples excited at 325 nm. Fig. 4 (a) Transient photocurrent density of m-CeO 2 , m-g-C 3 N 4 , and 3 m-CeO 2 g-C 3 N 4 electrodes at 0.3 V versus AgAgCl; (b) electrochemical impedance spectra of the as-prepared samples at A 0.4 V versus AgAgCl. Fig. 5 XPS profiles of (a) survey, (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, and (d) Ce 3d of the samples m-CeO 2 , m-g-C 3 N 4 and 3 m-CeO 2 g-C 3 N 4 . Fig. 6 Time-dependent CO (a), CH 4 (b) amounts generated on m-CeO 2 , m-g-C 3 N 4 , and m-CeO 2 g-C 3 N 4 nanocomposites.Stability studies of CO (c) and CH 4 (d) evolutions over 3 m-CeO 2 g-C 3 N 4 .Condition: 50 mg catalyst, reaction temperature 25 1C, ambient pressure, 300 W Xe-lamp. light response, highly efficient separation and transfer of photogenerated charge carriers across the interface between mesoporous CeO 2 and g-C 3 N 4 , and consequently, the greatly enhanced photocatalytic performance in the photoreduction of CO 2 by gaseous water under full spectrum light irradiation.A synergetic catalytic effect of mutual activations between m-CeO 2 and g-C 3 N 4 is proposed: The migration of photo-generated electrons from g-C 3 N 4 to m-CeO 2 and their trapping by Ce 4 leads to the recombination prevention of photogenerated electrons and holes in g-C 3 N 4 (g-C 3 N 4 activation) and the generation of Ce 3 from Ce 4 reduction by the trapped electrons in m-CeO 2 (CeO 2 activation), both of which benefits the activity enhancements of CO 2 reduction.As a result, as high as 0.590 mol CO and 0.694 mol CH 4 can be obtained by CO 2 reduction on 50 mg photocatalyst in the first hour.This work presents a promising m-CeO 2 g-C 3 N 4 photocatalyst for CO 2 photoconversion without addition of any noble-metal co-catalyst.Mengli Li received her M.S. degree from Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2010.Currently she is a Ph.D. candidate under the supervision of Professor Jianlin Shi at Shanghai Institute of Ceramics, Chinese Academy of Sciences.Her current main research focuses on the design and synthesis of graphitic carbon nitride based nanocomposites used in environmental protection and energy conversion.Lingxia Zhang received her Ph.D. degree from Shanghai Institute of Ceramics, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2003.She has been working at the institute after graduation.Currently her research mainly includes mesoporous and low-dimensional materials applied in artificial photosynthesis and environmental purification. Physicochemical properties and photocatalytic activities of the photocatalyst for CO 2 reduction under simulated solar light. a BET surface area.bBJHaverageporesize.cTheamount of CO evolution after 1 h irradiation.dTheamount of CH 4 evolution after 1 h irradiation. This work was financially supported by the National Key Basic Research Program of China (2013CB933200), National 863 plans projects (2012AA062703), National Natural Science Foundation of China (21177137), Youth Innovation Promotion Association CAS (2012200) and Shanghai Technical Platform for Testing and Characterization on Inorganic Materials (14DZ2292900). Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at http:dx.doi.org10.1016j.nanoen.2015.11.010.

reactor type.source: In a typical process, 50 mg photocatalyst was uniformly dispersed on a flat glass plate with the area fixed at 1.5 cm A 1.5 cm.
reactor type.context: All the chemicals are analytical grade and used as received without further purification.The nanocomposites m-CeO 2 g-C 3 N 4 were prepared as follows: 20.0 g urea and certain amount (x wt) of Ce(NO 3 ) 3 6H 2 O were dissolved in 45.0 g water dispersion with 40 12-nm SiO 2 particles (Ludox HS-40, Aldrich), which were used as hard template.The mixture was kept at 85 1C with magnetic stirring to evaporate water and then put in an oven at 80 1C overnight.The resultant white powder was calcined at 550 1C in an alumina crucible with a cover for 2 h in static air with a ramp rate of 4 1Cmin.The yellow product was finally treated with 4 M ammonium bifluoride (NH 4 HF 2 ) for 48 h to remove the silica template.The powder was then centrifuged and washed with ethanol and distilled water for several times and lyophilized overnight.The as-synthesized nanocomposites are denoted as x m-CeO 2 g-C 3 N 4 .The mesoporous g-C 3 N 4 (m-g-C 3 N 4 ) was prepared by the same method without adding Ce(NO 3 ) 3 6H 2 O.The mesoporous CeO 2 (m-CeO 2 ) can be obtained by removing g-C 3 N 4 from the m-CeO 2 g-C 3 N 4 nanocomposite under 550 1C for 3 h, as demonstrated by XPS analysis below. For photocatalyst characterization, X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD) were recorded by a Rigaku DMax 2200PC X-ray diffractometer with a scanning rate of 4 1min.The X-ray tube using Cu K radiation was operated at 40 mA and 40 kV.The measurement were made at room temperature in the 2 range of 10-801.Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area of the samples was characterized by sorption using Micromeritics Tristar 2000 instrument at 77 K. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns, and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of the catalyst samples were obtained by JEM-2100F field emission transmission electron microscope (FE-TEM) operated at 200 kV and equipped with an energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) instrument.Field emission scanning electron micrograph (FE-SEM) was acquired on a Hitachi S-4800.Ultraviolet visible (UV-vis) diffuse reflection spectra were recorded in the range of 200-800 nm on a UV-3101 PC Shimadzu spectroscope using BaSO 4 as a reflectance standard material.A Hitachi F-4500 was used to analyze the catalysts' photoluminescence (PL) characteristics with an excitation wavelength of 325 nm at room temperature.X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out on a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250 spectrometer with an Al K radiation line source and multichannel detector.All the binding energy values for the high-resolution spectra were calibrated with the signal of contaminated carbon C 1 s at 285 eV. Electrochemical measurements were carried out on a CHI 660A electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument, Inc.) with a standard three-electrode cell.Fluorine-doped Tin Oxide (FTO) deposited with photocatalysts served as a working electrode, while the counter and the reference electrodes were a platinum plate and AgAgCl respectively, with 0.2 M Na 2 SO 4 as electrolyte solution.The working electrodes were prepared by electrophoretic deposition in an acetone solution (25 mL) containing the sample powder (12 mg) and iodine (3 mg).Two parallel FTO glasses were kept in the above solution for 5 min with a 10 V bias under potentiostat control.All the samples are deposited as a thin film onto Fluorine-doped Tin Oxide (FTO), and the coated area was fixed at 1 cm 2 .After being calcined for 2 h in an oven at 150 1C, a homogeneous film can be obtained.50 mL of 0.2 M Na 2 SO 4 was used as electrolyte solution, which represented the best compromise between activity and stability of the nanocomposites.The transient photocurrent densities of the as-prepared electrodes were tested at 0.3 V versus AgAgCl under irradiation.The bias voltage for obtaining Nyquist plots of these electrodes was set at A 0.4 V versus AgAgCl in dark. CO 2 photocatalytic reduction was typically conducted in a gas-closed circulation system by using a reactor (volume: 500 mL) purchased from PerfectLight Company (China, Beijing).The reactor is made of borosilicate glass, which was purchased from Perfect Light Company (China, Beijing), with an optical quartz window at the top.To remove possible trace organic contaminants, all the samples were treated at 200 1C for 3 h in air.In a typical process, 50 mg photocatalyst was uniformly dispersed on a flat glass plate with the area fixed at 1.5 cm A 1.5 cm.In order to ensure that air was eliminated, high purity CO 2 (99.99) gas, monitoreded by a mass flow controller, was led to pass through a water bubbler to generate a mixture of CO 2 and water vapor.A 300 W of Xenon-arc lamp (Aulight CEL-HX, Beijing) was used as the light source.The temperature of the reactor was controlled at 25 1C by cooling water circulation.During the irradiation, 1 mL gas was sampled from the glass chamber at a given time intervals (1 h) for detecting CH 4 andor other carbon-based gases.CO can also be detected by installing a methanizer, using N 2 as carrier gas.The sample was analyzed by a gas chromatograph (GC-2060, Fuli Corp., China) with a flame ionization detector (FID) equipped with a 5A molecular column and a TDX-01 packed column.During the stability test, the photocatalyst was collected after each run and then reused for photoreduction of CO 2 .The catalyst was heattreated at 150 1C for two hours after the second and third runs before its reusing.
